name: Edgar
classification_notes: Edgar is explicitly described as an oldstyle text family, with letterforms drawing on the types of William Caslon and Alexander Phemister, placing it firmly in the humanist oldstyle serif tradition.
summary: An oldstyle text serif by Tobias Frere-Jones and Nina Stössinger, rooted in the British typographic tradition and named after Frere-Jones' great-grandfather, the crime novelist Edgar Wallace.
description: Designed as a sibling to Frere-Jones's Mallory sans, Edgar draws on the letterforms of William Caslon and Alexander Phemister to create a lively rhythm suited to long-form reading. Its origins lie in Frere-Jones's British ancestry and a desire to translate the conversational cadence of Edgar Wallace's crime thrillers into the shapes of the text itself. Ten styles run from Light to Black with matching italics across an extended Latin character set.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Edgar as an oldstyle text family designed as a sibling to their Mallory family, offering a lively rhythm for long-form reading while exploring the intertwining of personal and public histories. The design owes much to Tobias Frere-Jones's British ancestry and the British typographic tradition, beginning with his great-grandfather Edgar Wallace, the renowned early 20th-century thriller writer whose easy conversational rhythm Frere-Jones sought to replicate in the letterforms themselves, which touch on the types of William Caslon and Alexander Phemister.

name: Cassis
classification_notes: Cassis is described as a geometric sansserif informed by midcentury lettering and signpainting, with geometric underpinnings tempered by humanist personality through swelling curves and reaching terminals.
summary: A geometric sansserif by Nina Stössinger drawn from midcentury Belgian, Swiss and American lettering traditions, offered in seven weights from Thin to Black.
description: Cassis draws on early- to mid-century lettering and signpainting from Belgium, Switzerland and the United States, using geometric structure as a starting point rather than a constraint. Swelling curves, reaching terminals and a deliberate imbalance of stroke weights give it a warmth and density that work particularly well at display sizes. Seven weights from Thin through Black make it suited to branding, identity and titling where a strong but approachable voice is needed.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Cassis as a generous, soulful sansserif more interested in personality than perfection. Informed by early- through midcentury lettering and signpainting from Belgium, Switzerland and the United States, it draws from history and geometry without being limited by either, projecting affable confidence and compelling density at larger sizes through swelling curves, reaching terminals and a teetering balance of stroke weights.

name: Constrictor
classification_notes: Constrictor is a display sans that deliberately resists conventional contrast and style classification, with its defining characteristic being a continuous width axis spanning X-Wide to X-Condensed rather than a weight range.
summary: A single-weight variable display sans by Fred Shallcrass, built around a dramatic width axis that runs from X-Wide to X-Condensed across six static styles and one variable font.
description: Rather than varying weight, Constrictor varies width as its primary expressive dimension, shifting from an open, shimmering X-Wide to an almost unbearably compressed X-Condensed. Individual glyph shapes carry a sense of movement, and the family resists easy categorisation by mixing typographic history with associations drawn from the physical world. Swash alternates, oldstyle figures, ligatures and a raised colon feature among the OpenType options, with broad Latin coverage extending to Vietnamese and a range of minority languages.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Constrictor as a typeface in perpetual motion, designed by Fred Shallcrass. By defying strict contrast and style classifications, its patterns of interference are intended to provoke an emotional response and spark joy. Shallcrass layered typographic history with physical-world experiences and memories to create a family that radiates an energetic presence, with movement built into both individual glyphs and the family's overarching structure across its range from X-Wide to X-Condensed.

name: Supermassive
classification_notes: A cap-only display family of extreme weight and density, drawing on 1960s handlettering traditions with superelliptical curves; designed explicitly for headline and large-scale use with no text sizes.
summary: A cap-only display family in three styles by Tobias Frere-Jones, built around extreme weight and density drawn from 1960s handlettering traditions.
description: Three headline styles that push mass and density to an extreme, using long superelliptical curves and a deliberate tension between vertical and horizontal stroke weights. Minimal negative spaces bring the letterforms to a precarious heaviness while retaining enough idiosyncrasy to avoid the anonymity of generic ultra-bolds. Designed for headlines and larger, it works only in capitals, with contributions from Rosie Mai, Fred Shallcrass and Nina Stössinger.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Supermassive as hefty and idiosyncratic, squeezing maximal weight and density from the available space without sacrificing subtlety or soul. Responding to 1960s handlettering styles, it features long superelliptical curves and a teetering balance of vertical and horizontal weights, with minimal negative spaces pushing mass to a precarious extreme. Available in three cap-only styles drawn for headline sizes and larger.

name: Seaford
designer: Tobias Frere-Jones, Nina Stössinger, Fred Shallcrass
classification_notes: Seaford is described as rooted in long-form text serifs, with organic, gently asymmetric letterforms that resist modernist homogenisation — hallmarks of humanist sans design intended to aid legibility in extended reading.
summary: A text-focused humanist sans by Tobias Frere-Jones, Nina Stössinger and Fred Shallcrass, originally commissioned by Microsoft for Office 365, built around the rhythms of long-form serif typography.
description: Designed with gently asymmetric, organic letterforms that deliberately emphasise differences between individual letters, Seaford is intended to produce readable word shapes rather than pursue modernist uniformity. It comes in Standard and Display optical sizes, each with Regular and Bold weights plus italics, and offers four figure sets alongside OpenType alternates for differentiated or simplified forms. One of five new sans-serifs bundled with Office 365 subscriptions.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Seaford as a sturdy, sophisticated and energetic sans-serif for reading and writing, its design rooted in long-form text serifs to evoke familiarity and comfort. By resisting modernist homogenisation through organic, gently asymmetric forms, it emphasises distinctions between letters and creates recognisable word shapes. A range of alternates and four sets of figures increase its versatility; originally designed for Microsoft, it is one of five new sans-serifs included in Office 365.

name: Community Gothic
designer: Tobias Frere-Jones, Fred Shallcrass, Nina Stössinger
classification_notes: Rooted in nineteenth-century sans serif jobbing types, Community Gothic is a grotesque that deliberately revives the coarse, irregular forms of that genre rather than smoothing them into a refined contemporary grotesque.
summary: A deliberate revival of nineteenth-century jobbing grotesques by Tobias Frere-Jones, Fred Shallcrass and Nina Stössinger, built from mismatched historical sources to preserve the grit and irregularity of pre-digital type assembly.
description: Each of the ten styles was conceived and drawn independently, referencing American and European foundry sources, then integrated into a conventional weight-and-width progression for practical use. The designers built custom tools to pursue asymmetric curves and buckled lines, consciously resisting the uniformity typical of digitally drawn type. The result retains the coarseness of its nineteenth-century forebears while functioning as a coherent working family across five weights in normal and condensed widths.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Community Gothic as a revival of the sans serif jobbing typefaces of the nineteenth century, which were clear and durable but often coarse and unrelated to one another. Each style was drawn independently with reference to historical American and European foundry sources, using custom-built design tools to introduce asymmetric curves and buckled lines. The family consciously resists digital smoothness, producing what Frere-Jones call a powerful and emphatically human quality.

name: Interstate
classification_notes: Derived from Highway Gothic, the federal standard for American road signage, Interstate carries the open counters, uneven proportions and blunt terminals of a workhorse lettering system redrawn as a text and display typeface.
summary: A typeface drawn from Highway Gothic, the mid-century lettering system developed for the American Interstate Highway System, redrawn by Tobias Frere-Jones across 36 styles.
description: Frere-Jones took the unlikely source of Highway Gothic — the federal signage alphabet developed from 1949 by road engineers and sign painters — and rebuilt it as a full type family, preserving the cramped uppercase letters, the abrupt lowercase g descender and the blunt, stumbling charm of the originals. The family runs from Hairline to Ultra across Normal, Condensed and Compressed widths, with italics present across most weights. OpenType features including tabular figures, fractions and auxiliary figures are available in the core text weights.
foundry_blurb: Interstate began as a personal challenge to build a typeface from highway lettering, a source traditional type precepts would consider full of wrong shapes. Frere-Jones redesigned every original character and extrapolated missing glyphs such as ampersands and foreign accents, using a light touch to keep both the durability and the dissonant charm of Highway Gothic intact. The family was designed by Tobias Frere-Jones with contributions from Cyrus Highsmith, Nina Stössinger, Davy Dai and Richard Lipton.

name: Mallory
classification_notes: Mallory sits in the grotesque sans tradition, with a large family spanning multiple widths and optical sizes including a MicroPlus cut optimised for small text, characteristic of utilitarian grotesque design refined for demanding environments.
summary: A 110-style grotesque by Tobias Frere-Jones spanning five widths and two optical subfamilies, including a MicroPlus cut built for small-size legibility.
description: Mallory is an expansive grotesque sans designed by Tobias Frere-Jones, structured across Standard and MicroPlus subfamilies with widths running from regular through Compact, Narrow, Condensed and Extra Condensed. Eight weights from Thin to Ultra are paired with italics throughout, and variable fonts are available across both subfamilies. The MicroPlus cuts are specifically engineered for demanding small-text conditions, making the family as practical at caption size as it is at display scale.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones presents Mallory as a 110-style grotesque family available in variable font format, with Standard and MicroPlus subfamilies covering a wide range of widths and weights with full italic companions.

name: Nobel
designer: Tobias Frere-Jones after Sjoerd Hendrik de Roos
classification_notes: Nobel is a revival of Sjoerd Hendrik de Roos's 1929 adaptation of Berthold Grotesk, which tilted away from blunt 19th-century grotesques towards geometric severity, placing it between grotesque and geometric sans-serif traditions.
summary: Tobias Frere-Jones's revival of de Roos's 1929 Dutch grotesque, balancing geometric rigour with an approachable, slightly imperfect warmth.
description: Originally an adaptation of Berthold Grotesk made at Typefoundry Amsterdam in 1929, Nobel steers between the bluntness of 19th-century grotesques and the cold precision of Futura. Frere-Jones encountered a specimen by chance in 1992 and described the result as 'Futura cooked in dirty pots and pans', capturing its geometry tempered by human irregularity. The revival spans 18 styles across six weights in normal and condensed widths, with italic cuts for the upright series and contributions to the Extra Light from Cyrus Highsmith and Dyana Weissman.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Nobel as a revival of Sjoerd Hendrik de Roos's 1929 adaptation of Berthold Grotesk, which de Roos steered towards the severity of machine-age geometrics while retaining elegance and life. Tobias Frere-Jones found a specimen by chance in 1992 and began the revival as part of his RISD degree project, with new weights added over time and Extra Light styles contributed by Cyrus Highsmith and Dyana Weissman. The family now comprises 18 styles across normal and condensed widths.

name: Garage Gothic
classification_notes: Drawn from the crude, utilitarian letterforms of parking garage tickets, Garage Gothic sits firmly in the display category with its coarse geometry, rounded corners and truncated M and W forms derived from mechanical ephemeral printing traditions.
summary: A three-weight display family by Tobias Frere-Jones, derived from the crude numerical letterforms found on discarded parking garage tickets in 1980s Brooklyn.
description: Frere-Jones collected parking garage tickets intending to use them in collages, drawn to the matter-of-fact geometry of the printed numbers and the blurring caused by rapid mechanical printing. The truncated centres of M and W recall monospaced mechanical type, while rounded corners and joins make ink spread a design feature rather than a flaw. The family spans Regular, Bold and Black, with OpenType features for uppercase punctuation and a raised colon referencing the ticket-printing context.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Garage Gothic as exploring the coarse, unvarnished shapes of ephemeral printing, rooted in parking garage tickets Tobias Frere-Jones collected in Brooklyn during the 1980s. He expanded the source material into a full family with additional weights and hundreds of new characters, aiming to make a crude vernacular tradition as valid as classical typefaces and to document a genre largely absent from type history.

name: Griffith Gothic
classification_notes: Derived from C. H. Griffith's Bell Gothic, a 1937 American grotesque designed for telephone directories, expanded with additional weights, condensed widths and a new italic. The deliberately blunted joins and enlarged counterforms place it firmly in the grotesque tradition.
summary: An expansion of C. H. Griffith's Bell Gothic, the 1937 telephone-directory typeface, extended into a headline family with six weights, matching italics and a condensed series.
description: Originally commissioned by Patrick Mitchell at Fast Company, Griffith Gothic takes Bell Gothic's defensive design strategies, thinned joins, blunted corners and oversized counterforms, and builds them into an 18-style family suited to editorial display work. Fred Shallcrass subsequently revised and updated the family, adding refinements including contextual alternates for the 'f' and a set of OpenType features covering raised colons, alternate quotes and uppercase punctuation. The result makes available, at headline sizes, the idiosyncratic rhythm that graphic designers had long admired in the original six-to-eight-point directory face.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Griffith Gothic as a re-imagination of Bell Gothic, C. H. Griffith's 1937 custom design for telephone directories, pairing its classic American structure with unorthodox details. The family was first commissioned as a full headline series by Patrick Mitchell at Fast Company, and was later carefully revised and updated by Fred Shallcrass.

name: Magnet
classification_notes: Magnet combines an ultra-compressed headline sans with a workhorse text-capable sans under one family name; the Headline's extreme compression and theatrical proportions place it firmly in the display sans territory, while Standard operates as a conventional text sans.
summary: A two-part sans-serif family by Inga Plönnigs that pairs an ultra-compressed, theatrically tight Headline with a notch-detailed Standard built for dense text settings.
description: Designed by Inga Plönnigs with contributions from Nina Stössinger, Magnet splits into two distinct subfamilies: the Headline, offered in Upright, Slanted and Backslanted cuts, creates dense rhythmic patterns through extreme compression; the Standard runs across five weights from Light to Black with italics throughout, distinguished by deeply cut notches and boxy curves that suit tight leading and dense copy. The Headline's opposing slant directions give the family an unusual theatrical range rarely found in a single release. Both subfamilies share an underlying visual logic that makes them function together without feeling forced.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Magnet as an energetic and intriguing sans-serif family offering two functionally distinct subfamilies under one name. The Headline creates a dazzling pattern of tightly packed shapes, amplified by its Slanted and Backslanted styles, while the Standard carries its own flair through deeply cut notches and unusual weight transitions, with boxy curves suited to dense settings and tight line spacing. Plönnigs developed the family through years of experimenting with rigid patterns and organic details, aiming for a design that is both surprising and reliable.

name: Conductor
classification_notes: A display family with dramatic weight contrast and a dual roman/italic structure, rooted in vernacular lettering and mid-century type. The geometric grid-based construction and bold-only offering across widths place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A bold display family by Tobias Frere-Jones and Nina Stössinger, built on contrasting geometric structures drawn from Bulgarian lottery ticket numerals and vernacular shopfront lettering.
description: Running across four widths from Condensed to Wide, Conductor exists solely in Bold with matching italics, giving eight styles in total. The roman is constructed on a grid of rectangles while the italic is built around triangles, creating a retro-classical dyad where the two styles are complementary yet independently legible. Dramatic weight contrast and a rich set of OpenType alternates make it well suited to editorial headers, branding and packaging where a distinctive, energetic voice is required.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Conductor as a commanding display family inspired by diverse historical references and structural game-playing, originally based on delicate blocky numerals from vintage Bulgarian lottery tickets and incorporating vernacular shopfront lettering and mid-century type design. They characterise it as a dual family whose strong, graceful roman is built on rectangles and whose swift, energetic italic is built on triangles, noting that its seemingly self-contradictory approach produces something severe at first glance but dimensional in the details.

name: Empirica
classification_notes: Empirica draws on ancient Roman inscriptional lettering and 19th-century French revivals of that tradition, producing a sharp, high-contrast serif with close spacing suited to large display sizes.
summary: A sharp display serif by Tobias Frere-Jones and Nina Stössinger rooted in ancient Roman inscriptions and a 19th-century French revival of that tradition.
description: Originally commissioned for magazine headlines, Empirica takes its formal cues from Roman lapidary lettering and the work of French printer Louis Perrin, who reconstructed lost Lyon inscriptions in 1846. Close spacing and delicate serifs make it most at home in large sizes, where its six weights from Extra Light to Black give it a wide range from refined to forceful. Alternate short descenders allow tighter line spacing in headline settings, and an extensive set of OpenType features covers ligatures, oldstyle figures, fractions and contextual alternates.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Empirica as revisiting the grand style of Ancient Rome with a touch of French influence, built into a modern family projecting grace and authority. First commissioned for magazine headlines, its close spacing and delicate serifs suit large sizes, and its six weights with italics offer a full palette for editorial, branding and identity work.

name: Exchange
classification_notes: Exchange draws on early Ionic slab serif traditions and Victorian text faces for word-shape coherence, while incorporating lessons from Bell Gothic; its bracketed serifs and text-optimised proportions place it within the transitional/newspaper serif tradition rather than a pure slab category.
summary: A newspaper text face by Tobias Frere-Jones that draws on Ionic slab serifs and Victorian text traditions to hold legibility at small sizes across print and screen.
description: Originally drawn for newspaper columns, Exchange borrows its approach to word-shape coherence from early Ionic slabs and takes lessons in letter individuality from Bell Gothic. The compact proportions suit both narrow newspaper columns and mobile screens, and the MicroPlus subfamily extends its capability into digital text settings including web and video. Eighteen styles span two optical subfamilies, Standard and MicroPlus, each running from Light to Bold with italics throughout.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Exchange as a newspaper text face striving for clarity and efficient copyfit across multiple platforms, drawing on an unorthodox mix of historical references from nineteenth-century Britain to Depression-era America. The Ionic slab tradition supplies word-shape coherence, while Bell Gothic informs letter differentiation and Victorian text faces contribute a sure-footed sobriety. The MicroPlus addition extends the family into digital media, and the retail release expands the weight range and updates character sets to current standards.

name: Hightower
classification_notes: Hightower follows the Venetian style with a rising crossbar on the lowercase e, oldstyle figures and small capitals, placing it firmly in the humanist old-style tradition rooted in Jenson's work.
summary: A Venetian roman begun as an independent study at RISD and completed in 1994, named after former AIGA director Carolyn Hightower.
description: Begun as an independent study at Rhode Island School of Design when no formal type design instruction was available, Frere-Jones turned to Jenson as a starting point for understanding letterforms. Completed in 1994 for use in the AIGA Journal, the family comprises a roman and a matching italic, with the roman faithful to the Venetian style: rising crossbar on the lowercase e, oldstyle figures and small capitals. The italic takes a more open, contemporary approach rather than mimicking a strict chancery model.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Hightower as a true Venetian, with a rising crossbar on the lowercase e, oldstyle figures and small capitals. The typeface was started as an independent study at RISD, where Jenson's work served as a reference for understanding letterforms. Finished in 1994, it was first used in the AIGA Journal and named in honour of former AIGA director Carolyn Hightower. The italic is described as open and contemporary in style.

name: Grand Central
classification_notes: Rooted in the French Oldstyle tradition initiated by Louis Perrin in 1846, the design is a Beaux-Arts revival drawn directly from the sign lettering of Grand Central Terminal, placing it firmly within humanist old-style serif territory with a distinctly American inflection.
summary: A two-weight serif drawn from the original sign lettering of Grand Central Terminal, commissioned for the 1994 renovation to maintain continuity with the station's 1913 Beaux-Arts lettering.
description: Tobias Frere-Jones studied decades of archive photography to reconstruct the Light from the original American French Oldstyle lettering painted throughout Grand Central Terminal when it opened in 1913. The Bold was drawn to the specifications of the Americans with Disabilities Act, which governs contrast ratios and legibility requirements for public signage. OpenType features include contextual alternates for tighter letterpair forms, long-tailed Q variants, titling umlauts and arrow ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Grand Central traces its lineage to Louis Perrin's 1846 reinterpretation of ancient Roman lettering, a style later adopted by American sign painters for the notices and directions inside Grand Central Terminal. Frere-Jones was commissioned during the 1994 renovation to design a typeface matching the station's historic Beaux-Arts lettering, drawing the Light from archival photography and the Bold according to ADA signage standards. Contributions to the family were made by Devyani Mahadevan.

name: Retina
classification_notes: Retina is a sans-serif designed for small-size legibility in financial data and screen interfaces, with humanist proportions and deep ink-trap notches that clarify letterforms under difficult rendering conditions.
summary: A 72-style sans-serif by Tobias Frere-Jones built for extreme small-size legibility in financial data displays and screen interfaces, with deep ink-trap notches that reinforce letterform clarity under ink spread or low resolution.
description: Originally commissioned for The Wall Street Journal's stock listings, Retina addresses the specific problem of type that must remain readable at very small sizes in print and on screen. Deep notches cut into stroke junctions absorb ink spread and sharpen letterform distinctions that would otherwise collapse. The family spans two optical subfamilies, Standard and MicroPlus, each available in regular, narrow and condensed widths across up to seven weights with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Retina as a typeface where deep notches absorb ink in print and reinforce the gesture of each shape, designed for use in tabular forms and screen interfaces across 72 styles.

name: Interstate Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced adaptation of the Interstate grotesque family, preserving its highway-signage character while enforcing fixed-width metrics throughout all four weights.
summary: Christian Schwartz's monospaced reworking of Interstate, commissioned by a bank and preserving the original family's distinctive quirks in fixed-width form.
description: Originally commissioned for a bank requiring monospaced settings, this four-weight family adapts the central weights of Frere-Jones's Interstate grotesque into a fixed-pitch format. Schwartz retained Interstate's characteristic eccentricities drawn from US highway signage while introducing new ones appropriate to the monospaced context. The result sits naturally in technical, data-heavy and editorial applications where the warmth of a humanist grotesque is needed alongside strict column alignment.
foundry_blurb: On a commission from a bank, Christian Schwartz revised the central weights of the Interstate family for monospaced settings. The result is an eight-style family that preserves Interstate's eccentricities and introduces a few new ones.

name: Cafeteria
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a freeform sans serif; condensed but irregular in rhythm, inspired by hand lettering rather than systematic geometric or grotesque conventions.
summary: A freeform condensed sans drawn by Tobias Frere-Jones in 1992, inspired by the hand lettering of his friend Chris Vermaas, balancing visual activity with readability.
description: Begun as an experiment in rhythm and irregularity, Cafeteria avoids the stiffness typical of condensed sans serifs by introducing the organic variation found in hand lettering. Frere-Jones drew it with deliberate care to keep the liveliness from tipping into illegibility. Four weights from Light to Black make up the family, with no italics or condensed sub-cuts.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Cafeteria as originating in 1992 as an experiment in rhythm and irregularity, inspired largely by the hand lettering of his friend Chris Vermaas. They note that Frere-Jones took special care to balance activity with legibility, producing a freeform sans serif that is condensed but never stiff.

name: Niagara
classification_notes: A geometric all-caps display type in the Machine Age luxury tradition, with modular construction and an engraved variant; the tall, narrow letterforms and decorative inline effect place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A geometric display family by Tobias Frere-Jones that revives the Machine Age luxury aesthetic of pre-war America, offered in solid and engraved variants across three weights.
description: Drawing on a pre-war American genre that used geometry as a marker of luxury rather than precision, Niagara comes in five styles spanning Thin, Light and Regular weights, each available in Solid and Engraved cuts. The engraved effect lends an airy, inline quality that Frere-Jones links to the mist and romance of Niagara Falls. Small caps are drawn to match the lowercase height, enabling a stylised biform setting that plays into the family's inherent modularity.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Niagara as continuing a pre-war American genre where geometry conveyed Machine Age luxury rather than scientific precision. The small caps are drawn to the same height as the lowercase to allow a more stylised biform setting, and Tobias Frere-Jones connects the verticality of the letterforms to a waterfall's straight descent, and the engraved effect to Niagara Falls' image of bygone romance.

name: Epitaph
classification_notes: A revival of a nineteenth-century American Type Founders display face with characteristic Victorian inscriptional qualities, placing it squarely in the display category with a historical decorative subcategory.
summary: A single-style revival of a nineteenth-century American Type Founders display face, drawn by Tobias Frere-Jones from an old ATF specimen.
description: Drawn from an American Type Founders specimen, Epitaph captures the character of Victorian inscriptional lettering with a tension between harshness and softness that Frere-Jones describes as a fine line between quaint and loud. The family comprises a single Book weight with Latin script support across a wide range of Western European languages. Its narrow application makes it suited to headings, titling and editorial contexts where period flavour is the point.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describes Epitaph as sourced from an old American Type Founders specimen, drawn to understand a design that trod a fine line between the quaint and the powerful, moving from harshness to softness with no apparent conflict. Frere-Jones notes a personal attraction to nineteenth-century typography, having grown up in a city shaped by that era.

name: Citadel
classification_notes: A rigid, boxy display face drawing on German geometric display types like City and Stadion, offered in Solid and Inline cuts, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric subcategory.
summary: A boxy, forceful display face by Tobias Frere-Jones, offered in Solid and Inline cuts, drawing from German geometric display types like City and Stadion.
description: Developed as a variation of Frere-Jones's Niagara, Citadel pursues rigidity and aggression over delicacy, taking cues from German display designs with their squared, muscular forms. The Inline cut emerged from Frere-Jones's earlier work with Neville Brody on FF Tokyo, applying a decorative inline treatment to a potent structural foundation. Two styles, Book Solid and Book Inline, cover Latin-extended script across more than thirty languages.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describes Citadel as beginning as a variation of Niagara, aiming for something rigid and forceful rather than precious. Part of its DNA looks to German display designs like City and Stadion, with their boxy, aggressive character. The inline treatment was inspired by Frere-Jones's earlier collaboration with Neville Brody on FF Tokyo, applied here to give a potent design some added panache.

name: Pilsner
classification_notes: Pilsner bridges Roman and Blackletter traditions by eliminating curves entirely, replacing them with angular, faceted strokes. This places it firmly in the display category as a highly distinctive, historically referential design with no conventional serif or sans-serif structural basis.
summary: A faceted display face by Tobias Frere-Jones that eliminates all curves to bridge Roman and Blackletter traditions, with extra-long ascenders referencing Rudolf Koch.
description: Frere-Jones describes the design as stemming from a half-remembered beer label with lettering that read like a Roman letter wearing a Blackletter overcoat. By stripping out curvature entirely, the family draws on Vojtech Preissig's early experiments while the extended ascenders nod directly to Koch's mastery of both traditions. Four weights from Light to Black make it flexible for display use, but the unconventional structure keeps it firmly in expressive or editorial territory.
foundry_blurb: Tobias Frere-Jones designed Pilsner to bridge the divide between Roman and Blackletter traditions, inspired by a faceted beer label lettering style he once glimpsed. Questioning whether curvature was truly the barrier between the two traditions, he drew a family with no curves at all, referencing Vojtech Preissig's early faceted designs from the 1920s and giving the ascenders extra length as a nod to Rudolf Koch.

name: Asphalt
classification_notes: A heavy display-only sans in two styles, designed for large sizes with an expressive, animated quality referencing Excoffon's Antique Olive Nord. Its extreme weight and lively character place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A two-style display family by Tobias Frere-Jones, channelling the weight of Excoffon's Antique Olive Nord with a lively, animated rhythm suited only to large sizes.
description: Spun off from the Cafeteria family, Asphalt takes on the dense optical mass of Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive Nord and adds a playful, swinging energy that Frere-Jones himself describes as a "lively, if faintly inebriated, gyration". The family offers just two styles, Black and Condensed Black, making it a specialist tool for headlines and posters rather than a workhorse. Its narrow remit sharpens its voice considerably.
foundry_blurb: Frere-Jones describe Asphalt as a tangent from the Cafeteria family, taking on the weight of Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive Nord and a playful animation. Intended for large sizes only, it is characterised by a lively, if faintly inebriated, gyration.
